stories.headline: Trump declares US-Iran deal 'complete'; Hormuz to reopen, naval blockade to lift, frozen funds to be released
stories.summary: Trump declared the US-Iran agreement 'now complete' on June 14, ending hostilities 'on all fronts, including Lebanon' and reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Iran reopens Hormuz and lifts its naval blockade; the US releases ~$24bn in frozen assets and waives oil sanctions pending ~60 days of nuclear talks. An electronic signing and ceremony in Switzerland are set for June 19. As Monday markets opened, oil plunged about 5% to a three-month low and global equities surged.

perspectives.keyFraming: A war-ending framework reopens a critical oil chokepoint and roils markets, though full nuclear terms remain unsettled until the June 19 signing.
perspectives.summary: Reuters/AP-line reporting frames the announcement as a war-ending framework: Hormuz reopening alongside a lifted US blockade, about $24bn released, oil-sanction waivers and an Iranian non-nuclear pledge, with the MoU to be signed June 19 in Switzerland and implementation — mine-clearing, field restarts — still ahead.

sources.title: U.S. and Iran announce a deal to end the war, reopen Strait of Hormuz
U.S. and Iran reach deal to end the Mideast war, with agreement set to be signed Friday

perspectives.keyFraming: Chinese state media presents the deal as vindicating diplomacy, emphasizing Hormuz reopening for global shipping.
perspectives.summary: People's Daily reported Trump's claim the deal 'is now complete,' the strait reopening on signing and Iran's non-nuclear commitment, framing reopening as restoring global energy flows consistent with Beijing's interest in keeping Hormuz open.

sources.title: U.S.-Iran deal reached, Strait of Hormuz to reopen

perspectives.keyFraming: Deal ends the blockade but is fragile; Lebanon escalation could derail it and a final nuclear accord is unfinished.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL frames Trump's 'now complete' claim around the end of the oil blockade and Hormuz reopening while stressing the deal's preliminary nature and the risk from the parallel Israel-Hezbollah escalation.

sources.title: Trump Says Iran Deal 'Now Complete' — Blockade To End, Hormuz To Open

perspectives.keyFraming: Tehran casts the memorandum as consolidating a wartime victory, not a concession.
perspectives.summary: Iranian state media quoted Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi describing the memorandum with the US as the fruit of two months of negotiations aimed at 'consolidating the victory the Iranian people had already achieved,' a framing aimed at domestic audiences ahead of the signing.

sources.title: Araghchi says MoU with US will consolidate victory of Iranian people

stories.headline: Oil falls over 4% to lowest since March as US-Iran deal sets Strait of Hormuz reopening; global equities surge
stories.summary: Crude tumbled as Monday trading opened after the US and Iran confirmed a war-ending deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with an MoU signing set for June 19 in Switzerland. Brent fell about 4.7% toward $83 and WTI about 5% toward $80-81, both at roughly three-month lows on expectations of a supply surge. Global equities rallied — the Nikkei and Kospi jumped over 5% and S&P 500 futures rose — as the war premium unwound days before Kevin Warsh's first FOMC.

perspectives.keyFraming: A fifth of the world's seaborne oil flows through Hormuz; its reopening removes the single largest bullish factor of 2026.
perspectives.summary: Reuters-line reporting framed the sell-off as markets pricing a rapid return of barred Gulf barrels: Brent dropped about $4 to roughly $83 and WTI about 5% to the low $80s, the lowest since March, with the IEA's Oil Market Report due June 17 set to extend forecasts to 2027.

sources.title: Oil slips over 4% after US, Iran reach peace deal to reopen Strait of Hormuz

perspectives.keyFraming: Import-dependent Asian markets cheered the deal as relief for energy security, even amid supply-glut fears.
perspectives.summary: Asian-market coverage reported the Nikkei and Kospi surging more than 5% and crude hitting its lowest since March, framing the reopening as relief for import-dependent economies while traders warned of a glut as barred barrels return.

sources.title: Brent Crude Hits Lowest Since March as US-Iran Deal Triggers Supply Surge Fears

perspectives.keyFraming: European leaders welcomed the deal and urged rapid Hormuz reopening, signaling sanctions relief as the G7 opened.
perspectives.summary: World leaders welcomed the agreement as the G7 opened in Évian; the UK, France, Germany and Italy called it a moment of opportunity and urged 'unrestricted freedom of navigation' through Hormuz, with Europe signaling sanctions calibration as oil flows resume.

sources.title: World leaders welcome U.S.-Iran deal as Europe signals sanctions relief, urges Hormuz reopening

stories.headline: Warsh's first FOMC (June 16-17): near-certain hold, but the new dot plot is the main event
stories.summary: Kevin Warsh chairs his first FOMC on June 16-17 with markets pricing roughly 98% odds of a hold at 3.50-3.75%. The focus is the new dot plot and Warsh's tone: after May CPI hit 4.2% (a three-year high) and PPI 6.5%, analysts expect projections to show no further 2026 cuts, with some officials possibly penciling in a hike. May retail sales land June 17; US markets close June 19 for Juneteenth.

perspectives.keyFraming: The rate hold is a foregone conclusion; the signal is whether Warsh formally shelves cuts and the dots drift hawkish.
perspectives.summary: A hold is about 98% priced. The real questions are the dot plot's 2026 median — 4.2% CPI and 6.5% PPI argue for removing remaining cut projections — and whether Warsh, viewed as hawkish, leaves a window open or tilts the next move toward a hike.

sources.title: What To Expect at Kevin Warsh's First Federal Reserve Meeting as Chair
Fed Interest Rate Decision 2026: Powell Is Out, Warsh Is In

perspectives.keyFraming: A hawkish Warsh defending price stability is the responsible response to an oil-driven inflation spike.
perspectives.summary: Right-leaning framing welcomes a hawkish hold and a dot plot closing off 2026 cuts, arguing the Fed must not ease into 4.2% CPI, while a Hormuz-reopening Iran deal would unwind the energy shock and validate holding firm.

sources.title: Warsh Faces His First Test June 17 as Traders Hunt for Signals in the Fed's Dot Plot

stories.headline: Anthropic still locked out of Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for foreign users as it disputes the export order
stories.summary: Days after a Commerce Department export-control directive ordered Anthropic to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for any foreign national — including its own foreign-born staff — the company kept the models broadly disabled while disputing the basis. Officials cited a technique to bypass Fable 5 safeguards; Anthropic argues the jailbreak was narrow and says it is working to restore access. It remains the first export-control action against a deployed LLM.

perspectives.keyFraming: The precedent — the state switching off a live frontier model worldwide — outweighs the specifics of this one jailbreak.
perspectives.summary: Time, Fortune and Bloomberg reported that whatever the flaw's severity, the demonstrated authority to disable a deployed frontier model for all foreign users is the landmark issue; Anthropic chose a full shutdown because selective compliance would have blocked its own foreign-born engineers.

sources.title: Anthropic Pulls Its Most Powerful AI Models After U.S. Bars Foreign Access
Anthropic disables Fable and Mythos AI models following U.S. export ban

perspectives.keyFraming: National security first: the government acted on a dual-use model adept at finding software vulnerabilities.
perspectives.summary: Sympathetic coverage emphasizes legitimate state authority to restrict foreign access to a Mythos-class model unusually capable at identifying software flaws that could be weaponized as a cyber tool.

sources.title: US orders Anthropic to disable AI models for all foreign nationals

perspectives.keyFraming: Anthropic says the jailbreak was narrow and is complying under protest while seeking restoration.
perspectives.summary: Anthropic's statement said it had only verbal evidence of a narrow, non-universal jailbreak, believes the issue a misunderstanding, and is working to restore access, while warning the standard could halt frontier deployments if applied broadly.

sources.title: Statement on US export-control directive for Fable 5 and Mythos 5

stories.headline: Israel strikes Beirut's Dahiyeh, killing at least three, after Hezbollah projectile fire; south Lebanon displacement orders issued
stories.summary: Israel struck Hezbollah targets in Beirut's Dahiyeh suburb on June 14, killing at least three, in what Netanyahu and Defense Minister Katz called a response to drone and projectile fire on northern Israel. Israel also ordered the forced displacement of some 29 south Lebanon towns; the IDF said it notified US CENTCOM first. Iran warned the strike 'will not go unanswered,' raising fears for the just-announced US-Iran truce, which Tehran says covers Lebanon.

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel casts strikes as a defensive response to a Hezbollah ceasefire violation — war with Hezbollah, not Lebanon.
perspectives.summary: Netanyahu and Katz said the IDF struck Hezbollah targets in Dahiyeh in response to drone and projectile fire into Israeli territory, framing the campaign as targeting Hezbollah rather than the Lebanese state, and said CENTCOM was notified before the strike.

sources.title: Israel braces for Iranian missile fire after strike on Hezbollah target in Beirut

perspectives.keyFraming: Israeli strikes on a civilian area and mass displacement orders endanger Lebanese civilians and the truce.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera reports Israel issuing forced-displacement orders for 29 southern Lebanese towns and killing at least three in Dahiyeh, emphasizing civilian harm and the risk to the ceasefire and broader regional diplomacy.

sources.title: At least three killed as Israel attacks southern Beirut
